cwes.description: A static code block creates an instance of a class.

cwes.name: Invokable Control Element with Signature Containing an Excessive Number of Parameters
cwes.description:
The product contains a function, subroutine, or method whose signature has an unnecessarily large number of
					parameters/arguments.

cwes.name: Runtime Resource Management Control Element in a Component Built to Run on Application Servers
cwes.description: The product uses deployed components from application servers, but it also uses low-level functions/methods for management of resources, instead of the API provided by the application server.

cwes.name: Missing Serialization Control Element
cwes.description:
The product contains a serializable data element that does not
					have an associated serialization method.

cwes.name: Excessive Execution of Sequential Searches of Data Resource
cwes.description:
The product contains a data query against an SQL table or view
					that is configured in a way that does not utilize an index and may cause
					sequential searches to be performed.

cwes.name: Empty Exception Block
cwes.description: An invokable code block contains an exception handling block that does not contain any code, i.e. is empty.

cwes.name: Serializable Data Element Containing non-Serializable Item Elements
cwes.description:
The product contains a serializable, storable data element such as a field or member,
					but the data element contains member elements that are not
					serializable.

cwes.name: Empty Code Block
cwes.description: The source code contains a block that does not contain any code, i.e., the block is empty.

cwes.name: Data Resource Access without Use of Connection Pooling
cwes.description:
The product accesses a data resource through a database without using a
					connection pooling capability.

cwes.name: Class with Excessively Deep Inheritance
cwes.description:
A class has an inheritance level that is too high, i.e., it
					has a large number of parent classes.

cwes.name: Unconditional Control Flow Transfer outside of Switch Block
cwes.description:
The product performs unconditional control transfer (such as a
					"goto") in code outside of a branching structure such as a switch
					block.

cwes.name: Insufficient Adherence to Expected Conventions
cwes.description:
The product's architecture, source code, design, documentation,
					or other artifact does not follow required conventions.

cwes.name: Floating Point Comparison with Incorrect Operator
cwes.description:
The code performs a comparison such as an
        equality test between two float (floating point) values, but
        it uses comparison operators that do not account for the
        possibility of loss of precision.

cwes.name: Inappropriate Source Code Style or Formatting
cwes.description:
The source code does not follow
				desired style or formatting for indentation, white
				space, comments, etc.

cwes.name: Parent Class without Virtual Destructor Method
cwes.description: A parent class contains one or more child classes, but the parent class does not have a virtual destructor method.

cwes.name: Source Code File with Excessive Number of Lines of Code
cwes.description:
A source code file has too many lines of
					code.

cwes.name: Class Instance Self Destruction Control Element
cwes.description: The code contains a class instance that calls the method or function to delete or destroy itself.

cwes.name: Data Access from Outside Expected Data Manager Component
cwes.description: The product is intended to manage data access through a particular data manager component such as a relational or non-SQL database, but it contains code that performs data access operations without using that component.

cwes.name: Invokable Control Element with Excessive File or Data Access Operations
cwes.description:
A function or method contains too many
					operations that utilize a data manager or file resource.

cwes.name: Invokable Control Element with Excessive Volume of Commented-out Code
cwes.description:
A function, method, procedure, etc. contains an excessive amount of code that has been
					commented out within its body.

cwes.name: Synchronous Access of Remote Resource without Timeout
cwes.description: The code has a synchronous call to a remote resource, but there is no timeout for the call, or the timeout is set to infinite.

cwes.name: Large Data Table with Excessive Number of Indices
cwes.description:
The product uses a large data table that contains an excessively large number of
					indices.

cwes.name: Use of Same Invokable Control Element in Multiple Architectural Layers
cwes.description:
The product uses the same control element across multiple
					architectural layers.

cwes.name: Excessively Complex Data Representation
cwes.description: The product uses an unnecessarily complex internal representation for its data structures or interrelationships between those structures.

cwes.name: Excessive Index Range Scan for a Data Resource
cwes.description:
The product contains an index range scan for a large data table,
					but the scan can cover a large number of rows.

cwes.name: Loop Condition Value Update within the Loop
cwes.description:
The product uses a loop with a control flow condition based on
					a value that is updated within the body of the loop.

cwes.name: Singleton Class Instance Creation without Proper Locking or Synchronization
cwes.description: The product implements a Singleton design pattern but does not use appropriate locking or other synchronization mechanism to ensure that the singleton class is only instantiated once.

cwes.name: Persistent Storable Data Element without Associated Comparison Control Element
cwes.description:
The product uses a storable data element that does not have
					all of the associated functions or methods that are necessary to support
					comparison.

cwes.name: Data Element containing Pointer Item without Proper Copy Control Element
cwes.description: The code contains a data element with a pointer that does not have an associated copy or constructor method.

cwes.name: Inconsistent Naming Conventions for Identifiers
cwes.description:
The product's code, documentation, or other artifacts do not
					consistently use the same naming conventions for variables, callables, groups of
					related callables, I/O capabilities, data types, file names, or similar types of
					elements.

cwes.name: Insufficient Isolation of System-Dependent Functions
cwes.description:
The product or code does not isolate system-dependent
					functionality into separate standalone modules.

cwes.name: Reliance on Runtime Component in Generated Code
cwes.description:
The product uses automatically-generated code that cannot be
					executed without a specific runtime support component.

cwes.name: Reliance on Machine-Dependent Data Representation
cwes.description:
The code uses a data representation that relies on low-level
					data representation or constructs that may vary across different processors,
					physical machines, OSes, or other physical components.

cwes.name: Use of Platform-Dependent Third Party Components
cwes.description:
The product relies on third-party components that do
					not provide equivalent functionality across all desirable
					platforms.

cwes.name: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components
cwes.description:
The product relies on third-party components that are not
					actively supported or maintained by the original developer or a trusted proxy
					for the original developer.

cwes.name: Insufficient Encapsulation of Machine-Dependent Functionality
cwes.description:
The product or code uses machine-dependent functionality, but
					it does not sufficiently encapsulate or isolate this functionality from
					the rest of the code.

cwes.name: Insufficient Use of Symbolic Constants
cwes.description:
The source code uses literal constants that may need to change
					or evolve over time, instead of using symbolic constants.

cwes.name: Insufficient Isolation of Symbolic Constant Definitions
cwes.description:
The source code uses symbolic constants, but it does not
					sufficiently place the definitions of these constants into a more centralized or
					isolated location.

cwes.name: Excessive Reliance on Global Variables
cwes.description:
The code is structured in a way that relies too much on using
					or setting global variables throughout various points in the code, instead of
					preserving the associated information in a narrower, more local
					context.

cwes.name: Use of Same Variable for Multiple Purposes
cwes.description:
The code contains a callable, block, or other code element in
					which the same variable is used to control more than one unique task or store
					more than one instance of data.

cwes.name: Incomplete I/O Documentation
cwes.description:
The product's documentation does not adequately define inputs,
					outputs, or system/software interfaces.

cwes.name: Incomplete Documentation of Program Execution
cwes.description:
The document does not fully define all mechanisms that are used
					to control or influence how product-specific programs are
					executed.

cwes.name: Inappropriate Comment Style
cwes.description:
The source code uses comment styles or formats that are
					inconsistent or do not follow expected standards for the
					product.

cwes.name: Inappropriate Whitespace Style
cwes.description:
The source code contains whitespace that is inconsistent across
					the code or does not follow expected standards for the
					product.

cwes.name: Source Code Element without Standard Prologue
cwes.description:
The source code contains elements such as source files 
					that do not consistently provide a prologue or header that has been
					standardized for the project.

cwes.name: Inaccurate Source Code Comments
cwes.description:
The source code contains comments that do not accurately
					describe or explain aspects of the portion of the code with which the comment is
					associated.

cwes.name: Callable with Insufficient Behavioral Summary
cwes.description:
The code contains a function or method whose signature and/or associated
					inline documentation does not sufficiently describe the callable's inputs, outputs,
					side effects, assumptions, or return codes.

cwes.name: Insufficient Documentation of Error Handling Techniques
cwes.description:
The documentation does not sufficiently describe the techniques
					that are used for error handling, exception processing, or similar
					mechanisms.

cwes.name: Excessive Use of Unconditional Branching
cwes.description:
The code uses too many unconditional branches (such as
					"goto").

cwes.name: Excessive Code Complexity
cwes.description:
The code is too complex, as calculated using a well-defined,
					quantitative measure.

cwes.name: Excessive McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity
cwes.description:
The code contains McCabe cyclomatic complexity that exceeds a
	desirable maximum.

cwes.name: Excessive Halstead Complexity
cwes.description:
The code is structured in a way that a Halstead complexity
					measure exceeds a desirable maximum.

cwes.name: Excessive Use of Self-Modifying Code
cwes.description: The product uses too much self-modifying code.

cwes.name: Excessively Deep Nesting
cwes.description:
The code contains a callable or other code grouping in which
					the nesting / branching is too deep.

cwes.name: Excessive Attack Surface
cwes.description:
The product has an attack surface whose quantitative
					measurement exceeds a desirable maximum.

cwes.name: Declaration of Variable with Unnecessarily Wide Scope
cwes.description:
The source code declares a variable in one scope, but the
					variable is only used within a narrower scope.

cwes.name: Compilation with Insufficient Warnings or Errors
cwes.description:
The code is compiled without sufficient warnings enabled, which
					may prevent the detection of subtle bugs or quality
					issues.

cwes.name: Irrelevant Code
cwes.description:
The product contains code that is not essential for execution,
	     i.e. makes no state changes and has no side effects that alter
	     data or control flow, such that removal of the code would have no impact
	     to functionality or correctness.

cwes.name: Improper Use of Validation Framework
cwes.description: The product does not use, or incorrectly uses, an input validation framework that is provided by the source language or an independent library.

cwes.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Improper Model Validation
cwes.description: The ASP.NET application does not use, or incorrectly uses, the model validation framework.

cwes.name: Inefficient CPU Computation
cwes.description:
The product performs CPU computations using
         algorithms that are not as efficient as they could be for the
         needs of the developer, i.e., the computations can be
         optimized further.

cwes.name: Use of Prohibited Code
cwes.description:
The product uses a function, library, or third party component
	     that has been explicitly prohibited, whether by the developer or
	     the customer.

cwes.name: Initialization of a Resource with an Insecure Default
cwes.description: The product initializes or sets a resource with a default that is intended to be changed by the product's installer, administrator, or maintainer, but the default is not secure.

cwes.name: Improper Isolation of Shared Resources on System-on-a-Chip (SoC)
cwes.description: The System-On-a-Chip (SoC) does not properly isolate shared resources between trusted and untrusted agents.

cwes.name: DMA Device Enabled Too Early in Boot Phase
cwes.description: The product enables a Direct Memory Access (DMA) capable device before the security configuration settings are established, which allows an attacker to extract data from or gain privileges on the product.

cwes.name: On-Chip Debug and Test Interface With Improper Access Control
cwes.description: The chip does not implement or does not correctly perform access control to check whether users are authorized to access internal registers and test modes through the physical debug/test interface.

cwes.name: Improper Identifier for IP Block used in System-On-Chip (SOC)
cwes.description: The System-on-Chip (SoC) does not have unique, immutable identifiers for each of its components.

cwes.name: Power-On of Untrusted Execution Core Before Enabling Fabric Access Control
cwes.description: The product enables components that contain untrusted firmware before memory and fabric access controls have been enabled.

cwes.name: Generation of Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
cwes.description:
The product uses a cryptographic primitive that uses an Initialization
			Vector (IV), but the product does not generate IVs that are
			sufficiently unpredictable or unique according to the expected
			cryptographic requirements for that primitive.

cwes.name: Failure to Disable Reserved Bits
cwes.description: The reserved bits in a hardware design are not disabled prior to production. Typically, reserved bits are used for future capabilities and should not support any functional logic in the design.   However, designers might covertly use these bits to debug or further develop new capabilities in product…

cwes.name: Insufficient Granularity of Access Control
cwes.description: The product implements access controls via a policy or other feature with the intention to disable or restrict accesses (reads and/or writes) to assets in a system from untrusted agents. However, implemented access controls lack required granularity, which renders the control policy too broad becaus…

cwes.name: Insufficient Granularity of Address Regions Protected by Register Locks
cwes.description: The product defines a large address region protected from modification by the same register lock control bit. This results in a conflict between the functional requirement that some addresses need to be writable by software during operation and the security requirement that the system configuration…

cwes.name: Creation of Emergent Resource
cwes.description: The product manages resources or behaves in a way that indirectly creates a new, distinct resource that can be used by attackers in violation of the intended policy.

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information Through Metadata
cwes.description: The product prevents direct access to a resource containing sensitive information, but it does not sufficiently limit access to metadata that is derived from the original, sensitive information.

cwes.name: Improper Prevention of Lock Bit Modification
cwes.description: The product uses a trusted lock bit for restricting access to registers, address regions, or other resources, but the product does not prevent the value of the lock bit from being modified after it has been set.

cwes.name: Improper Lock Behavior After Power State Transition
cwes.description: Register lock bit protection disables changes to system configuration once the bit is set. Some of the protected registers or lock bits become programmable after power state transitions (e.g., Entry and wake from low power sleep modes) causing the system configuration to be changeable.

cwes.name: Security-Sensitive Hardware Controls with Missing Lock Bit Protection
cwes.description: The product uses a register lock bit protection mechanism, but it does not ensure that the lock bit prevents modification of system registers or controls that perform changes to important hardware system configuration.

cwes.name: Hardware Internal or Debug Modes Allow Override of Locks
cwes.description: System configuration protection may be bypassed during debug mode.

cwes.name: Incorrect Use of Autoboxing and Unboxing for Performance Critical Operations
cwes.description: The code uses boxed primitives, which may introduce inefficiencies into performance-critical operations.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Formula Elements in a CSV File
cwes.description: The product saves user-provided information into a Comma-Separated Value (CSV) file, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as a command when the file is opened by a spreadsheet product.

cwes.name: Improper Zeroization of Hardware Register
cwes.description: The hardware product does not properly clear sensitive information from built-in registers when the user of the hardware block changes.

cwes.name: Use of a Cryptographic Primitive with a Risky Implementation
cwes.description: To fulfill the need for a cryptographic primitive, the product implements a cryptographic algorithm using a non-standard, unproven, or disallowed/non-compliant cryptographic implementation.

cwes.name: Inclusion of Undocumented Features or Chicken Bits
cwes.description: The device includes chicken bits or undocumented features that can create entry points for unauthorized actors.

cwes.name: Sensitive Non-Volatile Information Not Protected During Debug
cwes.description: Access to security-sensitive information stored in fuses is not limited during debug.

cwes.name: Internal Asset Exposed to Unsafe Debug Access Level or State
cwes.description:
The product uses physical debug or test
        interfaces with support for multiple access levels, but it
        assigns the wrong debug access level to an internal asset,
        providing unintended access to the asset from untrusted debug
        agents.

cwes.name: Improper Finite State Machines (FSMs) in Hardware Logic
cwes.description: Faulty finite state machines (FSMs) in the hardware logic allow an attacker to put the system in an undefined state, to cause a denial of service (DoS) or gain privileges on the victim's system.

cwes.name: Improper Write Handling in Limited-write Non-Volatile Memories
cwes.description: The product does not implement or incorrectly implements wear leveling operations in limited-write non-volatile memories.

cwes.name: Improper Protection Against Voltage and Clock Glitches
cwes.description: The device does not contain or contains incorrectly implemented circuitry or sensors to detect and mitigate voltage and clock glitches and protect sensitive information or software contained on the device.

cwes.name: Semiconductor Defects in Hardware Logic with Security-Sensitive Implications
cwes.description: The security-sensitive hardware module contains semiconductor defects.

cwes.name: Application-Level Admin Tool with Inconsistent View of Underlying Operating System
cwes.description: The product provides an application for administrators to manage parts of the underlying operating system, but the application does not accurately identify all of the relevant entities or resources that exist in the OS; that is, the application's model of the OS's state is inconsistent with the OS's…

cwes.name: Improper Preservation of Consistency Between Independent Representations of Shared State
cwes.description: The product has or supports multiple distributed components or sub-systems that are each required to keep their own local copy of shared data - such as state or cache - but the product does not ensure that all local copies remain consistent with each other.

cwes.name: CPU Hardware Not Configured to Support Exclusivity of Write and Execute Operations
cwes.description: The CPU is not configured to provide hardware support for exclusivity of write and execute operations on memory. This allows an attacker to execute data from all of memory.

cwes.name: Incorrect Selection of Fuse Values
cwes.description: The logic level used to set a system to a secure state relies on a fuse being unblown.

cwes.name: Incorrect Comparison Logic Granularity
cwes.description: The product's comparison logic is performed over a series of steps rather than across the entire string in one operation. If there is a comparison logic failure on one of these steps, the operation may be vulnerable to a timing attack that can result in the interception of the process for nefarious…

cwes.name: Comparison Logic is Vulnerable to Power Side-Channel Attacks
cwes.description: A device's real time power consumption may be monitored during security token evaluation and the information gleaned may be used to determine the value of the reference token.

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of Software Interfaces to Hardware Features
cwes.description:
The product provides software-controllable
			device functionality for capabilities such as power and
			clock management, but it does not properly limit
			functionality that can lead to modification of
			hardware memory or register bits, or the ability to
			observe physical side channels.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control Applied to Mirrored or Aliased Memory Regions
cwes.description: Aliased or mirrored memory regions in hardware designs may have inconsistent read/write permissions enforced by the hardware. A possible result is that an untrusted agent is blocked from accessing a memory region but is not blocked from accessing the corresponding aliased memory region.

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive System Information Due to Uncleared Debug Information
cwes.description: The hardware does not fully clear security-sensitive values, such as keys and intermediate values in cryptographic operations, when debug mode is entered.

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of Security Token Assignment
cwes.description: The System-On-A-Chip (SoC) implements a Security Token mechanism to differentiate what actions are allowed or disallowed when a transaction originates from an entity. However, the Security Tokens are improperly protected.

cwes.name: Improper Handling of Overlap Between Protected Memory Ranges
cwes.description: The product allows address regions to overlap, which can result in the bypassing of intended memory protection.

cwes.name: Improper Handling of Single Event Upsets
cwes.description: The hardware logic does not effectively handle when single-event upsets (SEUs) occur.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control for Register Interface
cwes.description: The product uses memory-mapped I/O registers that act as an interface to hardware functionality from software, but there is improper access control to those registers.

cwes.name: Improper Physical Access Control
cwes.description: The product is designed with access restricted to certain information, but it does not sufficiently protect against an unauthorized actor with physical access to these areas.

cwes.name: Hardware Logic with Insecure De-Synchronization between Control and Data Channels
cwes.description: The hardware logic for error handling and security checks can incorrectly forward data before the security check is complete.

cwes.name: Unintended Reentrant Invocation of Non-reentrant Code Via Nested Calls
cwes.description: The product invokes code that is believed to be reentrant, but the code performs a call that unintentionally produces a nested invocation of the non-reentrant code.

cwes.name: Improper Scrubbing of Sensitive Data from Decommissioned Device
cwes.description: The product does not properly provide a capability for the product administrator to remove sensitive data at the time the product is decommissioned.  A scrubbing capability could be missing, insufficient, or incorrect.

cwes.name: Policy Uses Obsolete Encoding
cwes.description: The product uses an obsolete encoding mechanism to implement access controls.

cwes.name: Policy Privileges are not Assigned Consistently Between Control and Data Agents
cwes.description: The product's hardware-enforced access control for a particular resource improperly accounts for privilege discrepancies between control and write policies.

cwes.name: Generation of Incorrect Security Tokens
cwes.description: The product implements a Security Token mechanism to differentiate what actions are allowed or disallowed when a transaction originates from an entity. However, the Security Tokens generated in the system are incorrect.

cwes.name: Uninitialized Value on Reset for Registers Holding Security Settings
cwes.description: Security-critical logic is not set to a known value on reset.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control for Volatile Memory Containing Boot Code
cwes.description: The product conducts a secure-boot process that transfers bootloader code from Non-Volatile Memory (NVM) into Volatile Memory (VM), but it does not have sufficient access control or other protections for the Volatile Memory.

cwes.name: Sensitive Cookie with Improper SameSite Attribute
cwes.description: The SameSite attribute for sensitive cookies is not set, or an insecure value is used.

cwes.name: Hardware Child Block Incorrectly Connected to Parent System
cwes.description: Signals between a hardware IP and the parent system design are incorrectly connected causing security risks.

cwes.name: Firmware Not Updateable
cwes.description:
The product does not provide its
			users with the ability to update or patch its
			firmware to address any vulnerabilities or
			weaknesses that may be present.

cwes.name: Missing Protection Against Hardware Reverse Engineering Using Integrated Circuit (IC) Imaging Techniques
cwes.description: Information stored in hardware may be recovered by an attacker with the capability to capture and analyze images of the integrated circuit using techniques such as scanning electron microscopy.

cwes.name: Cryptographic Operations are run Before Supporting Units are Ready
cwes.description: Performing cryptographic operations without ensuring that the supporting inputs are ready to supply valid data may compromise the cryptographic result.

cwes.name: Access Control Check Implemented After Asset is Accessed
cwes.description: A product's hardware-based access control check occurs after the asset has been accessed.

cwes.name: Sequence of Processor Instructions Leads to Unexpected Behavior
cwes.description: Specific combinations of processor instructions lead to undesirable behavior such as locking the processor until a hard reset performed.

cwes.name: Assumed-Immutable Data is Stored in Writable Memory
cwes.description: Immutable data, such as a first-stage bootloader, device identifiers, and "write-once" configuration settings are stored in writable memory that can be re-programmed or updated in the field.

cwes.name: Mutable Attestation or Measurement Reporting Data
cwes.description: The register contents used for attestation or measurement reporting data to verify boot flow are modifiable by an adversary.

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Specified Quantity in Input
cwes.description: The product receives input that is expected to specify a quantity (such as size or length), but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the quantity has the required properties.

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Specified Index, Position, or Offset in Input
cwes.description: The product receives input that is expected to specify an index, position, or offset into an indexable resource such as a buffer or file, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the specified index/position/offset has the required properties.

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Syntactic Correctness of Input
cwes.description: The product receives input that is expected to be well-formed - i.e., to comply with a certain syntax - but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input complies with the syntax.

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Specified Type of Input
cwes.description: The product receives input that is expected to be of a certain type, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input is actually of the expected type.

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Consistency within Input
cwes.description: The product receives a complex input with multiple elements or fields that must be consistent with each other, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input is actually consistent.

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Unsafe Equivalence in Input
cwes.description: The product receives an input value that is used as a resource identifier or other type of reference, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input is equivalent to a potentially-unsafe value.

cwes.name: Incorrect Decoding of Security Identifiers
cwes.description: The product implements a decoding mechanism to decode certain bus-transaction signals to security identifiers. If the decoding is implemented incorrectly, then untrusted agents can now gain unauthorized access to the asset.

cwes.name: Public Key Re-Use for Signing both Debug and Production Code
cwes.description: The same public key is used for signing both debug and production code.

cwes.name: Incorrect Conversion of Security Identifiers
cwes.description: The product implements a conversion mechanism to map certain bus-transaction signals to security identifiers. However, if the conversion is incorrectly implemented, untrusted agents can gain unauthorized access to the asset.

cwes.name: Missing Source Correlation of Multiple Independent Data
cwes.description: The product relies on one source of data, preventing the ability to detect if an adversary has compromised a data source.

cwes.name: Insecure Security Identifier Mechanism
cwes.description: The System-on-Chip (SoC) implements a Security Identifier mechanism to differentiate what actions are allowed or disallowed when a transaction originates from an entity. However, the Security Identifiers are not correctly implemented.

cwes.name: Debug Messages Revealing Unnecessary Information
cwes.description: The product fails to adequately prevent the revealing of unnecessary and potentially sensitive system information within debugging messages.

cwes.name: Incorrect Chaining or Granularity of Debug Components
cwes.description: The product's debug components contain incorrect chaining or granularity of debug components.

cwes.name: Unprotected Confidential Information on Device is Accessible by OSAT Vendors
cwes.description: The product does not adequately protect confidential information on the device from being accessed by Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test (OSAT) vendors.

cwes.name: Missing Protection Mechanism for Alternate Hardware Interface
cwes.description:
The lack of protections on alternate paths to access
                control-protected assets (such as unprotected shadow registers
                and other external facing unguarded interfaces) allows an
                attacker to bypass existing protections to the asset that are
		only performed…

cwes.name: Improper Protection of Physical Side Channels
cwes.description:
The device does not contain sufficient protection
	mechanisms to prevent physical side channels from exposing
	sensitive information due to patterns in physically observable
	phenomena such as variations in power consumption,
	electromagnetic emissions (EME), or acoustic emissions.

cwes.name: Insufficient or Incomplete Data Removal within Hardware Component
cwes.description: The product's data removal process does not completely delete all data and potentially sensitive information within hardware components.

cwes.name: Missing Source Identifier in Entity Transactions on a System-On-Chip (SOC)
cwes.description: The product implements a security identifier mechanism to differentiate what actions are allowed or disallowed when a transaction originates from an entity. A transaction is sent without a security identifier.

cwes.name: Non-Transparent Sharing of Microarchitectural Resources
cwes.description: Hardware structures shared across execution contexts (e.g., caches and branch predictors) can violate the expected architecture isolation between contexts.

cwes.name: Improperly Preserved Integrity of Hardware Configuration State During a Power Save/Restore Operation
cwes.description:
The product performs a power save/restore
            operation, but it does not ensure that the integrity of
            the configuration state is maintained and/or verified between
	    the beginning and ending of the operation.

cwes.name: Missing Ability to Patch ROM Code
cwes.description: Missing an ability to patch ROM code may leave a System or System-on-Chip (SoC) in a vulnerable state.

cwes.name: Missing Protection for Mirrored Regions in On-Chip Fabric Firewall
cwes.description: The firewall in an on-chip fabric protects the main addressed region, but it does not protect any mirrored memory or memory-mapped-IO (MMIO) regions.

cwes.name: Hardware Allows Activation of Test or Debug Logic at Runtime
cwes.description: During runtime, the hardware allows for test or debug logic (feature) to be activated, which allows for changing the state of the hardware. This feature can alter the intended behavior of the system and allow for alteration and leakage of sensitive data by an adversary.

cwes.name: Missing Write Protection for Parametric Data Values
cwes.description: The device does not write-protect the parametric data values for sensors that scale the sensor value, allowing untrusted software to manipulate the apparent result and potentially damage hardware or cause operational failure.

cwes.name: Improper Setting of Bus Controlling Capability in Fabric End-point
cwes.description: The bus controller enables bits in the fabric end-point to allow responder devices to control transactions on the fabric.

cwes.name: Fabric-Address Map Allows Programming of Unwarranted Overlaps of Protected and Unprotected Ranges
cwes.description: The address map of the on-chip fabric has protected and unprotected regions overlapping, allowing an attacker to bypass access control to the overlapping portion of the protected region.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control in Fabric Bridge
cwes.description: The product uses a fabric bridge for transactions between two Intellectual Property (IP) blocks, but the bridge does not properly perform the expected privilege, identity, or other access control checks between those IP blocks.

cwes.name: Missing Support for Security Features in On-chip Fabrics or Buses
cwes.description: On-chip fabrics or buses either do not support or are not configured to support privilege separation or other security features, such as access control.

cwes.name: Improper Protection against Electromagnetic Fault Injection (EM-FI)
cwes.description: The device is susceptible to electromagnetic fault injection attacks, causing device internal information to be compromised or security mechanisms to be bypassed.

cwes.name: Improper Protection for Outbound Error Messages and Alert Signals
cwes.description: Untrusted agents can disable alerts about signal conditions exceeding limits or the response mechanism that handles such alerts.

cwes.name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype Attributes ('Prototype Pollution')
cwes.description: The product receives input from an upstream component that specifies attributes that are to be initialized or updated in an object, but it does not properly control modifications of attributes of the object prototype.

cwes.name: Use of Blocking Code in Single-threaded, Non-blocking Context
cwes.description:
The product uses a non-blocking model that relies on a single threaded process
			for features such as scalability, but it contains code that can block when it is invoked.

cwes.name: Improper Management of Sensitive Trace Data
cwes.description:
Trace data collected from several sources on the
                System-on-Chip (SoC) is stored in unprotected locations or
                transported to untrusted agents.

cwes.name: Improperly Controlled Sequential Memory Allocation
cwes.description: The product manages a group of objects or resources and performs a separate memory allocation for each object, but it does not properly limit the total amount of memory that is consumed by all of the combined objects.

cwes.name: Missing Immutable Root of Trust in Hardware
cwes.description: A missing immutable root of trust in the hardware results in the ability to bypass secure boot or execute untrusted or adversarial boot code.

cwes.name: Security Version Number Mutable to Older Versions
cwes.description: Security-version number in hardware is mutable, resulting in the ability to downgrade (roll-back) the boot firmware to vulnerable code versions.

cwes.name: Reliance on Component That is Not Updateable
cwes.description: The product contains a component that cannot be updated or patched in order to remove vulnerabilities or significant bugs.

cwes.name: Remanent Data Readable after Memory Erase
cwes.description: Confidential information stored in memory circuits is readable or recoverable after being cleared or erased.

cwes.name: Improper Isolation of Shared Resources in Network On Chip (NoC)
cwes.description: The Network On Chip (NoC) does not isolate or incorrectly isolates its on-chip-fabric and internal resources such that they are shared between trusted and untrusted agents, creating timing channels.

cwes.name: Improper Handling of Faults that Lead to Instruction Skips
cwes.description: The device is missing or incorrectly implements circuitry or sensors that detect and mitigate the skipping of security-critical CPU instructions when they occur.

cwes.name: Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity
cwes.description: The product uses a regular expression with an inefficient, possibly exponential worst-case computational complexity that consumes excessive CPU cycles.

cwes.name: Unauthorized Error Injection Can Degrade Hardware Redundancy
cwes.description: An unauthorized agent can inject errors into a redundant block to deprive the system of redundancy or put the system in a degraded operating mode.

cwes.name: Incorrect Bitwise Shift of Integer
cwes.description: An integer value is specified to be shifted by a negative amount or an amount greater than or equal to the number of bits contained in the value causing an unexpected or indeterminate result.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements Used in a Template Engine
cwes.description: The product uses a template engine to insert or process externally-influenced input, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements or syntax that can be interpreted as template expressions or other code directives when processed by the engine.

cwes.name: Improper Protections Against Hardware Overheating
cwes.description: A hardware device is missing or has inadequate protection features to prevent overheating.

cwes.name: Insufficient Precision or Accuracy of a Real Number
cwes.description: The product processes a real number with an implementation in which the number's representation does not preserve required accuracy and precision in its fractional part, causing an incorrect result.

cwes.name: Multiple Releases of Same Resource or Handle
cwes.description: The product attempts to close or release a resource or handle more than once, without any successful open between the close operations.

cwes.name: Information Exposure through Microarchitectural State after Transient Execution
cwes.description: The processor does not properly clear microarchitectural state after incorrect microcode assists or speculative execution, resulting in transient execution.

cwes.name: Improper Handling of Hardware Behavior in Exceptionally Cold Environments
cwes.description:
A hardware device, or the firmware running on it, is
                missing or has incorrect protection features to maintain
                goals of security primitives when the device is cooled below
                standard operating temperatures.

cwes.name: Missing Origin Validation in WebSockets
cwes.description: The product uses a WebSocket, but it does not properly verify that the source of data or communication is valid.

cwes.name: Insecure Operation on Windows Junction / Mount Point
cwes.description: The product opens a file or directory, but it does not properly prevent the name from being associated with a junction or mount point to a destination that is outside of the intended control sphere.

cwes.name: Incorrect Parsing of Numbers with Different Radices
cwes.description: The product parses numeric input assuming base 10 (decimal) values, but it does not account for inputs that use a different base number (radix).

cwes.name: Weak Authentication
cwes.description: The product uses an authentication mechanism to restrict access to specific users or identities, but the mechanism does not sufficiently prove that the claimed identity is correct.

cwes.name: Use of Weak Credentials
cwes.description: The product uses weak credentials (such as a default key or hard-coded password) that can be calculated, derived, reused, or guessed by an attacker.

cwes.name: Use of Default Credentials
cwes.description: The product uses default credentials (such as passwords or cryptographic keys) for potentially critical functionality.

cwes.name: Use of Default Password
cwes.description: The product uses default passwords for potentially critical functionality.

cwes.name: Use of Default Cryptographic Key
cwes.description: The product uses a default cryptographic key for potentially critical functionality.

cwes.name: Dependency on Vulnerable Third-Party Component
cwes.description: The product has a dependency on a third-party component that contains one or more known vulnerabilities.

cwes.name: Incorrect Initialization of Resource
cwes.description: The product attempts to initialize a resource but does not correctly do so, which might leave the resource in an unexpected, incorrect, or insecure state when it is accessed.

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information during Transient Execution
cwes.description: A processor event or prediction may allow incorrect operations (or correct operations with incorrect data) to execute transiently, potentially exposing data over a covert channel.

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information in Shared Microarchitectural Structures during Transient Execution
cwes.description:
A processor event may allow transient operations to access
			architecturally restricted data (for example, in another address
			space) in a shared microarchitectural structure (for example, a CPU
			cache), potentially exposing the data over a covert channel.

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information caused by Incorrect Data Forwarding during Transient Execution
cwes.description:
A processor event or prediction may allow incorrect or stale data to
		  be forwarded to transient operations, potentially exposing data over a
		  covert channel.

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information caused by Shared Microarchitectural Predictor State that Influences Transient Execution
cwes.description:
Shared microarchitectural predictor state may allow code to influence
				transient execution across a hardware boundary, potentially exposing
				data that is accessible beyond the boundary over a covert channel.

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Generative AI Output
cwes.description:
The product invokes a generative AI/ML
			component whose behaviors and outputs cannot be directly
			controlled, but the product does not validate or
			insufficiently validates the outputs to ensure that they
			align with the intended security, content, or privacy
			policy.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Input Used for LLM Prompting
cwes.description:
The product uses externally-provided data to build prompts provided to
large language models (LLMs), but the way these prompts are constructed
causes the LLM to fail to distinguish between user-supplied inputs and
developer provided system directives.

cwes.name: Reliance on HTTP instead of HTTPS
cwes.description: The product provides or relies on use of HTTP communications when HTTPS is available.

cwes.name: Missing Security-Relevant Feedback for Unexecuted Operations in Hardware Interface
cwes.description:
The product has a hardware interface that silently discards operations
			in situations for which feedback would be security-relevant, such as
			the timely detection of failures or attacks.

cwes.name: Driving Intermediate Cryptographic State/Results to Hardware Module Outputs
cwes.description:
The product uses a hardware module implementing a cryptographic
		  algorithm that writes sensitive information about the intermediate
		  state or results of its cryptographic operations via one of its output
		  wires (typically the output port containing the final result).

cwes.name: Insecure Setting of Generative AI/ML Model Inference Parameters
cwes.description:
The product has a component that relies on a
	  generative AI/ML model configured with inference parameters that
	  produce an unacceptably high rate of erroneous or unexpected
	  outputs.
